title: Cat or Kitten Eyes Crusted Shut: Causes, Urgency, and Care
meta_desc: Learn why your kitten's eyes are crusted shut, the urgency of veterinary care, and how providing a photo can help with a faster diagnosis.
content_html:
What is causing my cat's eyes to be crusted shut?

When a cat or kitten has eyes that are matted or crusted shut, it is most often due to an Upper Respiratory Infection (URI) or feline conjunctivitis. These conditions cause discharge that, as it dries, acts like a glue sealing the eyelids together. This is frequently seen in kittens because their immune systems are still developing and they are more susceptible to viral and bacterial pathogens.

Is it an emergency?

This condition is classified as a Medium Urgency level. While it is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it requires a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours. If the infection is left untreated, it can lead to painful corneal ulcers, permanent scarring, or even the loss of the eye. Seek immediate care if your cat is also lethargic, refusing to eat, or having difficulty breathing.

Why a photo helps triage

Providing a high-quality photo to your veterinarian or triage assistant is incredibly beneficial. A visual allows professionals to assess the color and thickness of the discharge, the level of swelling in the conjunctiva, and whether the eye itself appears cloudy or red. This information helps the clinical team determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen and which diagnostic tests to prepare for.

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops on my kitten?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human eye drops or medications on a cat. Many human products contain ingredients that are toxic or irritating to feline eyes. Only use medications prescribed by a veterinarian.

faqs.question: How can I safely clean my cat's eyes at home?
faqs.answer: You can gently use a soft cloth or cotton ball dampened with lukewarm water to soften and wipe away the crusts. Use a fresh cloth for each eye to prevent spreading the infection between them.

faqs.question: Is this condition contagious to other pets?
faqs.answer: Yes, most feline respiratory and eye infections are highly contagious to other cats. It is best to isolate the affected cat and wash your hands thoroughly after handling them or cleaning their eyes.
